Simandl and Hrabe were also pedagogues whose method books and studies remain in use in the 2000s.

#### 1900s–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: 1900s–present")]

The leading figure of the double bass in the early 20th century was [Serge Koussevitzky](/wiki/Serge_Koussevitzky "Serge Koussevitzky"), best known as conductor of the [Boston Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Boston_Symphony_Orchestra "Boston Symphony Orchestra"), who popularized the double bass in modern times as a solo instrument. Because of improvements to the double bass with steel strings and better set-ups, the bass is now played at a more advanced level than ever before and more and more composers have written works for the double bass. In the mid-century and in the following decades, many new concerti were written for the double bass, including [Nikos Skalkottas](/wiki/Nikos_Skalkottas "Nikos Skalkottas")'s Concerto (1942), [Eduard Tubin](/wiki/Eduard_Tubin "Eduard Tubin")'s Concerto (1948), [Lars-Erik Larsson](/wiki/Lars-Erik_Larsson "Lars-Erik Larsson")'s Concertino (1957), [Gunther Schuller](/wiki/Gunther_Schuller "Gunther Schuller")'s Concerto (1962), [Hans Werner Henze](/wiki/Hans_Werner_Henze "Hans Werner Henze")'s Concerto (1966) and [Frank Proto](/wiki/Frank_Proto "Frank Proto")'s Concerto No. 1 (1968).

The *Solo For Contrabass* is one of the parts of [John Cage](/wiki/John_Cage "John Cage")'s *Concert For Piano And Orchestra* and can be played as a solo, or with any of the other parts both orchestral and/or piano. Similarly, his solo contrabass parts for the orchestral work *Atlas Eclipticalis* can also be performed as solos. Cage's indeterminate works such as *Variations I*, *Variations II*, *Fontana Mix*, *Cartridge Music* et al. can be arranged for a solo contrabassist. His work *26.1.1499 for a String Player* is often realized by a solo contrabass player, although it can also be played by a violinist, violist, or cellist.

From the 1960s through the end of the century [Gary Karr](/wiki/Gary_Karr "Gary Karr") was the leading proponent of the double bass as a solo instrument and was active in commissioning or having hundreds of new works and concerti written especially for him. Karr was given Koussevitzky's famous solo double bass by Olga Koussevitsky and played it in concerts around the world for 40 years before, in turn, giving the instrument to the [International Society of Bassists](/wiki/International_Society_of_Bassists "International Society of Bassists") for talented soloists to use in concert. Another important performer in this period, [Bertram Turetzky](/wiki/Bertram_Turetzky "Bertram Turetzky"), commissioned and premiered more than 300 double bass works.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Sergei_Koussevitsky.jpg/330px-Sergei_Koussevitsky.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sergei_Koussevitsky.jpg)

[Serge Koussevitzky](/wiki/Serge_Koussevitzky "Serge Koussevitzky") popularized the double bass in modern times as a solo instrument.

In the 1970s, 1980 and 1990s, new concerti included [Nino Rota](/wiki/Nino_Rota "Nino Rota")'s *Divertimento for Double Bass and Orchestra* (1973), [Alan Ridout](/wiki/Alan_Ridout "Alan Ridout")'s concerto for double bass and strings (1974), [Jean Françaix](/wiki/Jean_Fran%C3%A7aix "Jean Françaix")'s Concerto (1975), [Frank Proto](/wiki/Frank_Proto "Frank Proto")'s Concerto No. 2, [Einojuhani Rautavaara](/wiki/Einojuhani_Rautavaara "Einojuhani Rautavaara")'s *Angel of Dusk* (1980), [Gian Carlo Menotti](/wiki/Gian_Carlo_Menotti "Gian Carlo Menotti")'s Concerto (1983), [Christopher Rouse](/wiki/Christopher_Rouse_(composer) "Christopher Rouse (composer)")'s Concerto (1985), [Henry Brant](/wiki/Henry_Brant "Henry Brant")'s Ghost Nets (1988) and Frank Proto's "Carmen Fantasy for Double Bass and Orchestra" (1991) and "Four Scenes after Picasso" Concerto No. 3 (1997). [Peter Maxwell Davies](/wiki/Peter_Maxwell_Davies "Peter Maxwell Davies")' lyrical [Strathclyde Concerto](/wiki/Strathclyde_Concerto "Strathclyde Concerto") No. 7, for double bass and orchestra, dates from 1992.

In the first decade of the 21st century, new concerti include Frank Proto's "Nine Variants on Paganini" (2002), [Kalevi Aho](/wiki/Kalevi_Aho "Kalevi Aho")'s Concerto (2005), [John Harbison](/wiki/John_Harbison "John Harbison")'s *Concerto for Bass Viol* (2006), [André Previn](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Previn "André Previn")'s Double Concerto for violin, double bass, and orchestra (2007) and [John Woolrich](/wiki/John_Woolrich "John Woolrich")'s *To the Silver Bow*, for double bass, viola and strings (2014).

[Reinhold Glière](/wiki/Reinhold_Gli%C3%A8re "Reinhold Glière") wrote an Intermezzo and Tarantella for double bass and piano, Op. 9, No. 1 and No. 2 and a Praeludium and Scherzo for double bass and piano, Op. 32 No. 1 and No. 2. [Paul Hindemith](/wiki/Paul_Hindemith "Paul Hindemith") wrote a rhythmically challenging Double Bass Sonata in 1949. Frank Proto wrote his Sonata "1963" for Double Bass and Piano. In the Soviet Union, [Mieczysław Weinberg](/wiki/Mieczys%C5%82aw_Weinberg "Mieczysław Weinberg") wrote his Sonata No. 1 for double bass solo in 1971. [Giacinto Scelsi](/wiki/Giacinto_Scelsi "Giacinto Scelsi") wrote two double bass pieces called *Nuits* in 1972, and then in 1976, he wrote *Maknongan*, a piece for any low-voiced instrument, such as double bass, [contrabassoon](/wiki/Contrabassoon "Contrabassoon"), or tuba. [Vincent Persichetti](/wiki/Vincent_Persichetti "Vincent Persichetti") wrote solo works—which he called "Parables"—for many instruments. He wrote Parable XVII for Double Bass, Op. 131 in 1974. [Sofia Gubaidulina](/wiki/Sofia_Gubaidulina "Sofia Gubaidulina") penned a Sonata for double bass and piano in 1975. In 1976 American minimalist composer [Tom Johnson](/wiki/Tom_Johnson_(composer) "Tom Johnson (composer)") wrote "Failing – a very difficult piece for solo string bass" in which the player has to perform an extremely virtuosic solo on the bass whilst simultaneously reciting a text which says how very difficult the piece is and how unlikely he or she is to successfully complete the performance without making a mistake.

In 1977 Dutch-Hungarian composer [Géza Frid](/wiki/G%C3%A9za_Frid "Géza Frid") wrote a set of variations on The Elephant from [Saint-Saëns](/wiki/Saint-Sa%C3%ABns "Saint-Saëns")' *[Le Carnaval des Animaux](/wiki/Le_Carnaval_des_Animaux "Le Carnaval des Animaux")* for [scordatura](/wiki/Scordatura "Scordatura") double bass and string orchestra. In 1987 [Lowell Liebermann](/wiki/Lowell_Liebermann "Lowell Liebermann") wrote his Sonata for Contrabass and Piano Op. 24. Fernando Grillo wrote the "Suite No. 1" for double bass (1983/2005). [Jacob Druckman](/wiki/Jacob_Druckman "Jacob Druckman") wrote a piece for solo double bass entitled *Valentine*. US double bass soloist and composer [Bertram Turetzky](/wiki/Bertram_Turetzky "Bertram Turetzky") (born 1933) has performed and recorded more than 300 pieces written by and for him. He writes chamber music, baroque music, classical, jazz, [renaissance music](/wiki/Renaissance_music "Renaissance music"), improvisational music and world music

US minimalist composer [Philip Glass](/wiki/Philip_Glass "Philip Glass") wrote a prelude focused on the lower register that he scored for timpani and double bass. Italian composer [Sylvano Bussotti](/wiki/Sylvano_Bussotti "Sylvano Bussotti"), whose composing career spans from the 1930s to the first decade of the 21st century, wrote a solo work for bass in 1983 entitled *Naked Angel Face per contrabbasso*. Fellow Italian composer [Franco Donatoni](/wiki/Franco_Donatoni "Franco Donatoni") wrote a piece called *Lem for contrabbasso* in the same year. In 1989, French composer [Pascal Dusapin](/wiki/Pascal_Dusapin "Pascal Dusapin") (born 1955) wrote a solo piece called *In et Out* for double bass. In 1996, the Sorbonne-trained Lebanese composer [Karim Haddad](/wiki/Karim_Haddad "Karim Haddad") composed *Ce qui dort dans l'ombre sacrée* ("He who sleeps in the sacred shadows") for Radio France's Presence Festival. [Renaud Garcia-Fons](/wiki/Renaud_Garcia-Fons "Renaud Garcia-Fons") (born 1962) is a French double bass player and composer, notable for drawing on jazz, folk, and Asian music for recordings of his pieces like *Oriental Bass* (1997).

Two significant recent works written for solo bass include, [Mario Davidovsky](/wiki/Mario_Davidovsky "Mario Davidovsky")'s Synchronisms No.11 for double bass and electronic sounds and [Elliott Carter](/wiki/Elliott_Carter "Elliott Carter")'s Figment III, for solo double bass. The German composer [Gerhard Stäbler](/wiki/Gerhard_St%C3%A4bler "Gerhard Stäbler") wrote *Co-wie Kobalt* (1989–90), "...a music for double bass solo and grand orchestra". [Charles Wuorinen](/wiki/Charles_Wuorinen "Charles Wuorinen") added several important works to the repertoire, *Spinoff* trio for double bass, violin and conga drums, and *Trio for Bass Instruments* double bass, tuba and bass trombone, and in 2007 *Synaxis* for double bass, horn, oboe and clarinet with timpani and strings. The suite "Seven Screen Shots" for double bass and piano (2005) by Ukrainian composer [Alexander Shchetynsky](/wiki/Alexander_Shchetynsky "Alexander Shchetynsky") has a solo bass part that includes many unconventional methods of playing. The German composer [Claus Kühnl](/wiki/Claus_K%C3%BChnl "Claus Kühnl") wrote *Offene Weite / Open Expanse* (1998) and *Nachtschwarzes Meer, ringsum…* (2005) for double bass and piano.In 1997 [Joel Quarrington](/wiki/Joel_Quarrington "Joel Quarrington") commissioned the American / Canadian composer [Raymond Luedeke](/wiki/Raymond_Luedeke "Raymond Luedeke") to write his "Concerto for Double Bass and Orchestra", a piece he performed with the [Toronto Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Toronto_Symphony_Orchestra "Toronto Symphony Orchestra"), with the [Saskatoon Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Saskatoon_Symphony_Orchestra "Saskatoon Symphony Orchestra"), and, in a version for small orchestra, with the [Nova Scotia Symphony Orchestra](/w/index.php?title=Nova_Scotia_Symphony_Orchestra&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nova Scotia Symphony Orchestra (page does not exist)").[[33]](#cite_note-33) Composer Raymond Luedeke also composed a work for double bass, flute, and viola with narration, "The Book of Questions", with text by [Pablo Neruda](/wiki/Pablo_Neruda "Pablo Neruda").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In 2004 Italian double bassist and composer [Stefano Scodanibbio](/wiki/Stefano_Scodanibbio "Stefano Scodanibbio") made a double bass arrangement of [Luciano Berio](/wiki/Luciano_Berio "Luciano Berio")'s 2002 solo cello work *[Sequenza XIV](/wiki/Sequenza_XIV "Sequenza XIV")* with the new title *Sequenza XIVb*.

### Chamber music with double bass

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Chamber music with double bass")]

Since there is no established instrumental ensemble that includes the double bass, its use in chamber music has not been as exhaustive as the literature for ensembles such as the [string quartet](/wiki/String_quartet "String quartet") or [piano trio](/wiki/Piano_trio "Piano trio"). Despite this, there is a substantial number of [chamber works](https://web.archive.org/web/20161003155641/http://www.paulnemeth.com/basschamber.htm) that incorporate the double bass in both small and large ensembles.

There is a small body of works written for [piano quintet](/wiki/Piano_quintet "Piano quintet") with the instrumentation of piano, violin, viola, cello, and double bass. The most famous is [Franz Schubert](/wiki/Franz_Schubert "Franz Schubert")'s Piano Quintet in A major, known as "The [Trout Quintet](/wiki/Trout_Quintet "Trout Quintet")" for its set of variations in the fourth movement of Schubert's *[Die Forelle](/wiki/Die_Forelle "Die Forelle")*. Other works for this instrumentation written from roughly the same period include those by [Johann Nepomuk Hummel](/wiki/Johann_Nepomuk_Hummel "Johann Nepomuk Hummel"), [George Onslow](/wiki/George_Onslow_(composer) "George Onslow (composer)"), [Jan Ladislav Dussek](/wiki/Jan_Ladislav_Dussek "Jan Ladislav Dussek"), [Louise Farrenc](/wiki/Louise_Farrenc "Louise Farrenc"), [Ferdinand Ries](/wiki/Ferdinand_Ries "Ferdinand Ries"), [Franz Limmer](/wiki/Franz_Limmer "Franz Limmer"), [Johann Baptist Cramer](/wiki/Johann_Baptist_Cramer "Johann Baptist Cramer"), and [Hermann Goetz](/wiki/Hermann_Goetz "Hermann Goetz"). Later composers who wrote chamber works for this quintet include [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams"), [Colin Matthews](/wiki/Colin_Matthews "Colin Matthews"), [Jon Deak](/wiki/Jon_Deak "Jon Deak"), Frank Proto, and [John Woolrich](/wiki/John_Woolrich "John Woolrich"). Slightly larger sextets written for piano, string quartet, and double bass have been written by [Felix Mendelssohn](/wiki/Felix_Mendelssohn "Felix Mendelssohn"), [Mikhail Glinka](/wiki/Mikhail_Glinka "Mikhail Glinka"), [Richard Wernick](/wiki/Richard_Wernick "Richard Wernick"), and [Charles Ives](/wiki/Charles_Ives "Charles Ives").

In the genre of string quintets, there are a few works for string quartet with double bass. [Antonín Dvořák](/wiki/Anton%C3%ADn_Dvo%C5%99%C3%A1k "Antonín Dvořák")'s String Quintet in G major, Op.77 and [Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart](/wiki/Wolfgang_Amadeus_Mozart "Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart")'s Serenade in G major, K.525 ("[Eine kleine Nachtmusik](/wiki/Eine_kleine_Nachtmusik "Eine kleine Nachtmusik")") are the most popular pieces in this repertoire, along with works by [Miguel del Águila](/wiki/Miguel_del_%C3%81guila "Miguel del Águila") (*Nostalgica* for string quartet and bass), [Darius Milhaud](/wiki/Darius_Milhaud "Darius Milhaud"), [Luigi Boccherini](/wiki/Luigi_Boccherini "Luigi Boccherini") (3 quintets), [Harold Shapero](/wiki/Harold_Shapero "Harold Shapero"), and [Paul Hindemith](/wiki/Paul_Hindemith "Paul Hindemith"). Another example is Alistair Hinton's String Quintet (1969–77), which also includes a major part for solo soprano; at almost 170 minutes in duration, it is almost certainly the largest such work in the repertoire.

Slightly smaller string works with the double bass include six string sonatas by [Gioachino Rossini](/wiki/Gioachino_Rossini "Gioachino Rossini"), for two violins, cello, and double bass written at the age of twelve over the course of three days in 1804. These remain his most famous instrumental works and have also been adapted for wind quartet. Rossini and Dragonetti composed duos for cello and double bass, as did [Johannes Matthias Sperger](/wiki/Johannes_Matthias_Sperger "Johannes Matthias Sperger"), a major soloist on the "Viennese" tuning instrument of the 18th century. [Franz Anton Hoffmeister](/wiki/Franz_Anton_Hoffmeister "Franz Anton Hoffmeister") wrote four String Quartets for Solo Double Bass, Violin, Viola, and Cello in D Major. Frank Proto has written a Trio for Violin, Viola and Double Bass (1974), 2 Duos for Violin and Double Bass (1967 and 2005), and *The Games of October* for Oboe/English Horn and Double Bass (1991).

Larger works that incorporate the double bass include [Beethoven](/wiki/Beethoven "Beethoven")'s Septet in E♭ major, Op. 20, one of his most famous pieces during his lifetime, which consists of clarinet, horn, bassoon, violin, viola, cello, and bass. When the clarinetist [Ferdinand Troyer](/wiki/Ferdinand_Troyer "Ferdinand Troyer") commissioned a work from [Franz Schubert](/wiki/Franz_Schubert "Franz Schubert") for similar forces, he added one more violin for his Octet in F major, D.803. [Paul Hindemith](/wiki/Paul_Hindemith "Paul Hindemith") used the same instrumentation as Schubert for his own Octet. In the realm of even larger works, Mozart included the double bass in addition to 12 wind instruments for his "[Gran Partita](/wiki/Gran_Partita "Gran Partita")" Serenade, K.361 and [Martinů](/wiki/Bohuslav_Martin%C5%AF "Bohuslav Martinů") used the double bass in his [nonet](/wiki/Nonet_(music) "Nonet (music)") for wind quintet, violin, viola, cello and double bass.

Other examples of chamber works that use the double bass in mixed ensembles include [Sergei Prokofiev](/wiki/Sergei_Prokofiev "Sergei Prokofiev")'s Quintet in G minor, Op. 39 for oboe, clarinet, violin, viola, and double bass; [Miguel del Águila](/wiki/Miguel_del_%C3%81guila "Miguel del Águila")'s *Malambo* for bass flute and piano and for string quartet, bass and bassoon; [Erwin Schulhoff](/wiki/Erwin_Schulhoff "Erwin Schulhoff")'s Concertino for flute/piccolo, viola, and double bass; Frank Proto's *Afro-American Fragments* for bass clarinet, cello, double bass and narrator and Sextet for clarinet and strings; [Fred Lerdahl](/wiki/Fred_Lerdahl "Fred Lerdahl")'s Waltzes for violin, viola, cello, and double bass; [Mohammed Fairouz](/wiki/Mohammed_Fairouz "Mohammed Fairouz")'s Litany for double bass and wind quartet; [Mario Davidovsky](/wiki/Mario_Davidovsky "Mario Davidovsky")'s Festino for guitar, viola, cello, and double bass; and [Iannis Xenakis](/wiki/Iannis_Xenakis "Iannis Xenakis")'s Morsima-Amorsima for piano, violin, cello, and double bass. There are also new music ensembles that utilize the double bass such as Time for Three and [PROJECT Trio](/wiki/PROJECT_Trio "PROJECT Trio").

### Orchestral passages and solos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Orchestral passages and solos")]

The opening of the third movement of Beethoven's Symphony No. 5 is often used as an orchestral excerpt during bass [auditions](/wiki/Audition "Audition").

In the baroque and classical periods, composers typically had the double bass double the cello part in orchestral passages. A notable exception is Haydn, who composed solo passages for the double bass in his Symphonies No. 6 *Le Matin*, No. 7 *Le midi*, No. 8 *Le Soir*, No. 31 *Horn Signal*, and No. 45 *Farewell*—but who otherwise grouped bass and cello parts together. Beethoven paved the way for separate double bass parts, which became more common in the romantic era. The [scherzo](/wiki/Scherzo "Scherzo") and [trio](/wiki/Trio_(music) "Trio (music)") from [Beethoven's Fifth Symphony](/wiki/Beethoven%27s_Fifth_Symphony "Beethoven's Fifth Symphony") are famous orchestral excerpts, as is the recitative at the beginning of the fourth movement of [Beethoven's Ninth Symphony](/wiki/Beethoven%27s_Ninth_Symphony "Beethoven's Ninth Symphony"). In many nineteenth century symphonies and concertos, the typical impact of separate bass and cello parts was that bass parts became simpler and cello parts got the melodic lines and rapid passage work.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A double bass section of a modern [orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra") typically uses six to eight double bassists, usually in [unison](/wiki/Unison "Unison"). Smaller orchestras may have four double basses, and in exceptional cases, bass sections may have as many as ten members. If some double bassists have low C extensions, and some have regular (low E) basses, those with the low C extensions may play some passages an octave below the regular double basses. Also, some composers write divided (divisi) parts for the basses, where upper and lower parts in the music are often assigned to "outside" (nearer the audience) and "inside" players. Composers writing divisi parts for bass often write [perfect intervals](/wiki/Perfect_interval "Perfect interval"), such as octaves and fifths, but in some cases use thirds and sixths.

Where a composition calls for a solo bass part, the principal bass invariably plays that part. The section leader (or principal) also determines the bowings, often based on bowings set out by the concertmaster. In some cases, the principal bass may use a slightly different bowing than the concertmaster, to accommodate the requirements of playing bass. The principal bass also leads entrances for the bass section, typically by lifting the bow or plucking hand before the entrance or indicating the entrance with the head, to ensure the section starts together. Major professional orchestras typically have an assistant principal bass player, who plays solos and leads the bass section if the principal is absent.

While orchestral bass solos are somewhat rare, there are some notable examples. [Johannes Brahms](/wiki/Johannes_Brahms "Johannes Brahms"), whose father was a double bass player, wrote many difficult and prominent parts for the double bass in his symphonies. [Richard Strauss](/wiki/Richard_Strauss "Richard Strauss") assigned the double bass daring parts, and his symphonic poems and operas stretch the instrument to its limits. "The Elephant" from [Camille Saint-Saëns](/wiki/Camille_Saint-Sa%C3%ABns "Camille Saint-Saëns")' *[The Carnival of the Animals](/wiki/The_Carnival_of_the_Animals "The Carnival of the Animals")* is a satirical portrait of the double bass, and American virtuoso [Gary Karr](/wiki/Gary_Karr "Gary Karr") made his televised debut playing "The Swan" (originally written for the cello) with the [New York Philharmonic](/wiki/New_York_Philharmonic "New York Philharmonic") conducted by [Leonard Bernstein](/wiki/Leonard_Bernstein "Leonard Bernstein"). The third movement of [Gustav Mahler](/wiki/Gustav_Mahler "Gustav Mahler")'s [first symphony](/wiki/Symphony_No._1_(Mahler) "Symphony No. 1 (Mahler)") features a solo for the double bass that quotes the children's song *Frere Jacques*, transposed into a minor key. [Sergei Prokofiev](/wiki/Sergei_Prokofiev "Sergei Prokofiev")'s *[Lieutenant Kijé](/wiki/Lieutenant_Kij%C3%A9_(Prokofiev) "Lieutenant Kijé (Prokofiev)") Suite* features a difficult and very high double bass solo in the "Romance" movement. [Benjamin Britten](/wiki/Benjamin_Britten "Benjamin Britten")'s *[The Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra](/wiki/The_Young_Person%27s_Guide_to_the_Orchestra "The Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra")* contains a prominent passage for the double bass section.

### Double bass ensembles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Double bass ensembles")]

Ensembles made up entirely of double basses, though relatively rare, also exist, and several composers have written or arranged for such ensembles. Compositions for four double basses exist by [Gunther Schuller](/wiki/Gunther_Schuller "Gunther Schuller"), [Jacob Druckman](/wiki/Jacob_Druckman "Jacob Druckman"), [James Tenney](/wiki/James_Tenney "James Tenney"), [Claus Kühnl](/wiki/Claus_K%C3%BChnl "Claus Kühnl"), Robert Ceely, [Jan Alm](http://www.janalm.se/), Bernhard Alt, Norman Ludwin, Frank Proto, Joseph Lauber, [Erich Hartmann](/wiki/Erich_Hartmann_(musician) "Erich Hartmann (musician)"), [Colin Brumby](/wiki/Colin_Brumby "Colin Brumby"), Miloslav Gajdos and Theodore Albin Findeisen. [David A. Jaffe](/wiki/David_A._Jaffe "David A. Jaffe")'s "Who's on First?",[[35]](#cite_note-35) commissioned by the Russian National Orchestra is scored for five double basses. [Bertold Hummel](/wiki/Bertold_Hummel "Bertold Hummel") wrote a *Sinfonia piccola*[[36]](#cite_note-36) for eight double basses. Larger ensemble works include [Galina Ustvolskaya](/wiki/Galina_Ustvolskaya "Galina Ustvolskaya")'s Composition No. 2, "Dies Irae" (1973), for eight double basses, piano, and wooden cube, [José Serebrier](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Serebrier "José Serebrier")'s "George and Muriel" (1986), for solo bass, double bass ensemble, and chorus, and Gerhard Samuel's *What of my music!* (1979), for soprano, percussion, and 30 double basses.

Double bass ensembles include L'Orchestre de Contrebasses (6 members),[[37]](#cite_note-37) Bass Instinct (6 members),[[38]](#cite_note-38) Bassiona Amorosa (6 members),[[39]](#cite_note-39) the Chicago Bass Ensemble (4+ members),[[40]](#cite_note-40) [Ludus Gravis](http://www.ludusgravis.com/index.html) founded by Daniele Roccato and [Stefano Scodanibbio](/wiki/Stefano_Scodanibbio "Stefano Scodanibbio"), The Bass Gang (4 members),[[41]](#cite_note-41) the [London Double Bass Ensemble](/wiki/London_Double_Bass_Ensemble "London Double Bass Ensemble") (6 members) founded by members of the Philharmonia Orchestra of London who produced the LP[[42]](#cite_note-42) [Music Interludes by London Double Bass Ensemble](http://www.discogs.com/London-Double-Bass-Ensemble-Jim-Lawless-Steve-Gray-Music-Interludes/release/2008247) on [Bruton Music records](/wiki/Bruton_Music "Bruton Music"), Brno Double Bass Orchestra (14 members) founded by the double bass professor at [Janáček Academy of Music and Performing Arts](/wiki/Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek_Academy_of_Music_and_Performing_Arts "Janáček Academy of Music and Performing Arts") and principal double bass player at [Brno Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Brno_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Brno Philharmonic Orchestra") – Miloslav Jelinek, and the ensembles of [Ball State University](/wiki/Ball_State_University "Ball State University") (12 members), [Shenandoah University](/wiki/Shenandoah_University "Shenandoah University"), and the [Hartt School of Music](/wiki/Hartt_School_of_Music "Hartt School of Music"). The Amarillo Bass Base of [Amarillo, Texas](/wiki/Amarillo,_Texas "Amarillo, Texas") once featured 52 double bassists,[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) and The London Double Bass Sound, who have released a CD on Cala Records, have 10 players.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

In addition, the double bass sections of some orchestras perform as an ensemble, such as the [Chicago Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Chicago_Symphony_Orchestra "Chicago Symphony Orchestra")'s Lower Wacker Consort.[[46]](#cite_note-46) There is an increasing number of published compositions and arrangements for double bass ensembles, and the [International Society of Bassists](/wiki/International_Society_of_Bassists "International Society of Bassists") regularly features double bass ensembles (both smaller ensembles as well as very large "mass bass" ensembles) at its conferences, and sponsors the biennial David Walter Composition Competition, which includes a division for double bass ensemble works.

## Use in jazz

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Use in jazz")]

Main article: [Jazz bass](/wiki/Jazz_bass "Jazz bass")

Beginning around 1890, the early New Orleans jazz ensemble (which played a mixture of marches, [ragtime](/wiki/Ragtime "Ragtime"), and [Dixieland](/wiki/Dixieland "Dixieland")) was initially a marching band with a tuba or [sousaphone](/wiki/Sousaphone "Sousaphone") (or occasionally [bass saxophone](/wiki/Bass_saxophone "Bass saxophone")) supplying the bass line. As the music moved into bars and brothels, the upright bass gradually replaced these wind instruments around the 1920s.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Many early bassists doubled on both the *brass bass ([tuba](/wiki/Tuba "Tuba"))* and *string bass*, as the instruments were then often referred to. Bassists played improvised ["walking" bass lines](/wiki/Walking_bassline "Walking bassline")—scale- and arpeggio-based lines that outlined the [chord progression](/wiki/Chord_progression "Chord progression").

Because an unamplified upright bass is generally the quietest instrument in a jazz band, many players of the 1920s and 1930s used the *slap style*, slapping and pulling the strings to produce a rhythmic "slap" sound against the fingerboard. The slap style cuts through the sound of a band better than simply plucking the strings, and made the bass more easily heard on early sound recordings, as the recording equipment of that time did not favor low frequencies.[[48]](#cite_note-fotos-48) For more about the slap style, see *Modern playing styles*, below.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Minugs_1976.jpg/250px-Minugs_1976.jpg)](/wiki/File:Minugs_1976.jpg)

Jazz bassist [Charles Mingus](/wiki/Charles_Mingus "Charles Mingus") was also an influential bandleader and composer whose musical interests spanned from bebop to free jazz.

Jazz bass players are expected to improvise an accompaniment line or solo for a given chord progression. They are also expected to know the rhythmic patterns that are appropriate for different styles (e.g., Afro-Cuban). Bassists playing in a big band must also be able to read written-out bass lines, as some arrangements have written bass parts.

Many upright bass players have contributed to the evolution of jazz. Examples include swing era players such as [Jimmy Blanton](/wiki/Jimmy_Blanton "Jimmy Blanton"), who played with [Duke Ellington](/wiki/Duke_Ellington "Duke Ellington"), and [Oscar Pettiford](/wiki/Oscar_Pettiford "Oscar Pettiford"), who pioneered the instrument's use in [bebop](/wiki/Bebop "Bebop"). [Paul Chambers](/wiki/Paul_Chambers "Paul Chambers") (who worked with [Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis") on the famous *[Kind of Blue](/wiki/Kind_of_Blue "Kind of Blue")* album) achieved renown for being one of the first jazz bassists to play bebop solos with the bow. [Terry Plumeri](/wiki/Terry_Plumeri "Terry Plumeri") furthered the development of arco (bowed) solos, achieving horn-like technical freedom and a clear, vocal bowed tone, while [Charlie Haden](/wiki/Charlie_Haden "Charlie Haden"), best known for his work with [Ornette Coleman](/wiki/Ornette_Coleman "Ornette Coleman"), defined the role of the bass in [Free Jazz](/wiki/Free_Jazz "Free Jazz").

A number of other bassists, such as [Ray Brown](/wiki/Ray_Brown_(musician) "Ray Brown (musician)"), [Slam Stewart](/wiki/Slam_Stewart "Slam Stewart") and [Niels-Henning Ørsted Pedersen](/wiki/Niels-Henning_%C3%98rsted_Pedersen "Niels-Henning Ørsted Pedersen"), were central to the history of jazz. Stewart, who was popular with the beboppers, played his solos with a bow combined with octave humming. Notably, [Charles Mingus](/wiki/Charles_Mingus "Charles Mingus") was a highly regarded composer as well as a bassist noted for his technical virtuosity and powerful sound.[[49]](#cite_note-49) [Scott LaFaro](/wiki/Scott_LaFaro "Scott LaFaro") influenced a generation of musicians by liberating the bass from contrapuntal "walking" behind soloists instead favoring interactive, conversational melodies.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Since the commercial availability of [bass amplifiers](/wiki/Bass_amplifier "Bass amplifier") in the 1950s, jazz bassists have used amplification to augment the natural volume of the instrument.

While the electric bass guitar was used intermittently in jazz as early as 1951, beginning in the 1970s bassist [Bob Cranshaw](/wiki/Bob_Cranshaw "Bob Cranshaw"), playing with saxophonist [Sonny Rollins](/wiki/Sonny_Rollins "Sonny Rollins"), and fusion pioneers [Jaco Pastorius](/wiki/Jaco_Pastorius "Jaco Pastorius") and [Stanley Clarke](/wiki/Stanley_Clarke "Stanley Clarke") began to commonly substitute the bass guitar for the upright bass. Apart from the jazz styles of jazz fusion and Latin-influenced jazz however, the upright bass is still the dominant bass instrument in jazz. The sound and tone of the plucked upright bass is distinct from that of the fretted bass guitar. The upright bass produces a different sound than the bass guitar, because its strings are not stopped by metal [frets](/wiki/Fret "Fret"), instead having a continuous tonal range on the uninterrupted fingerboard. As well, bass guitars usually have a solid wood body, which means that their sound is produced by electronic amplification of the vibration of the strings, instead of the upright bass's acoustic reverberation.

Demonstrative examples of the sound of a solo double bass and its technical use in jazz can be heard on the solo recordings *[Emerald Tears](/wiki/Emerald_Tears "Emerald Tears")* (1978) by [Dave Holland](/wiki/Dave_Holland_(bassist) "Dave Holland (bassist)") or *[Emergence](/wiki/Emergence_(Miroslav_Vitou%C5%A1_album) "Emergence (Miroslav Vitouš album)")* (1986) by [Miroslav Vitouš](/wiki/Miroslav_Vitou%C5%A1 "Miroslav Vitouš"). Holland also recorded an album with the representative title *[Music from Two Basses](/wiki/Music_from_Two_Basses "Music from Two Basses")* (1971) on which he plays with [Barre Phillips](/wiki/Barre_Phillips "Barre Phillips") while he sometimes switches to cello.

## Use in bluegrass and country

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Use in bluegrass and country")]

See also: [List of double bassists in popular music](/wiki/List_of_double_bassists_in_popular_music "List of double bassists in popular music")

The string bass is the most commonly used bass instrument in bluegrass music and is almost always plucked, though some modern bluegrass bassists have also used a bow. The bluegrass bassist is part of the rhythm section, and is responsible for keeping a steady beat, whether fast, slow, in **4**  
**4**, **2**  
**4** or **3**  
**4** time. The bass also maintains the chord progression and harmony. The Engelhardt-Link (formerly [Kay](/wiki/Kay_Musical_Instrument_Company "Kay Musical Instrument Company")) brands of plywood laminate basses have long been popular choices for bluegrass bassists. Most bluegrass bassists use the 3⁄4 size bass, but the full-size and 5⁄8 size basses are also used.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Double_bass_Uncle_Charlie%27s_Smokehouse_Crozet_VA_June_2008.jpg/250px-Double_bass_Uncle_Charlie%27s_Smokehouse_Crozet_VA_June_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Double_bass_Uncle_Charlie%27s_Smokehouse_Crozet_VA_June_2008.jpg)

Upright bass used by a bluegrass group; the cable for a piezoelectric pickup can be seen extending from the bridge.

Early pre-bluegrass traditional music was often accompanied by the cello. The cellist Natalie Haas points out that in the US, you can find "...old photographs, and even old recordings, of American string bands with cello". However, "The cello dropped out of sight in folk music, and became associated with the orchestra."[[51]](#cite_note-51) The cello did not reappear in bluegrass until the 1990s and first decade of the 21st century. Some contemporary bluegrass bands favor the electric bass, because it is easier to transport than the large and somewhat fragile upright bass. However, the bass guitar has a different musical sound. Many musicians feel the slower attack and percussive, woody tone of the upright bass gives it a more "earthy" or "natural" sound than an electric bass, particularly when gut strings are used.

Common rhythms in bluegrass bass playing involve (with some exceptions) plucking on beats 1 and 3 in **4**  
**4** time; beats 1 and 2 in **2**  
**4** time, and on the downbeat in **3**  
**4** time (waltz time). Bluegrass bass lines are usually simple, typically staying on the root and fifth of each chord throughout most of a song. There are two main exceptions to this rule. Bluegrass bassists often do a diatonic *walkup* or *[walkdown](/wiki/Walkdown "Walkdown"),* in which they play every beat of a bar for one or two bars, typically when there is a chord change. In addition, if a bass player is given a solo, they may play a [walking bass](/wiki/Walking_bass "Walking bass") line with a note on every beat or play a pentatonic scale-influenced bassline.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Riders_Bass.jpg/250px-Riders_Bass.jpg)](/wiki/File:Riders_Bass.jpg)

Country music bassist "Too Slim" ([Fred LaBour](/wiki/Fred_LaBour "Fred LaBour") of [Riders in the Sky](/wiki/Riders_in_the_Sky_(band) "Riders in the Sky (band)")) performing in Ponca City, Oklahoma, in 2008

An early bluegrass bassist to rise to prominence was Howard Watts (also known as Cedric Rainwater), who played with [Bill Monroe](/wiki/Bill_Monroe "Bill Monroe")'s Blue Grass Boys beginning in 1944.[[52]](#cite_note-watts-52) The classical bassist [Edgar Meyer](/wiki/Edgar_Meyer "Edgar Meyer") has frequently branched out into [newgrass](/wiki/Progressive_bluegrass "Progressive bluegrass"), old-time, jazz, and other genres.
"My all-time favorite is [Todd Phillips](/wiki/Todd_Phillips_(musician) "Todd Phillips (musician)")", proclaimed Union Station bassist Barry Bales in April 2005. "He brought a completely different way of thinking about and playing bluegrass.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

An upright bass was the standard bass instrument in traditional [country western music](/wiki/Country_western_music "Country western music"). While the upright bass is still occasionally used in [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"), the electric bass has largely replaced its bigger cousin in country music, especially in the more pop-infused country styles of the 1990s and 2000s, such as new country.

### Slap-style bass

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Slap-style bass")]

Slap-style bass is sometimes used in bluegrass bass playing. When bluegrass bass players slap the string by pulling it until it hits the fingerboard or hit the strings against the fingerboard, it adds the high-pitched percussive "clack" or "slap" sound to the low-pitched bass notes, sounding much like the clacks of a tap dancer. Slapping is a subject of minor controversy in the bluegrass scene. Even slapping experts such as [Mike Bub](/wiki/Mike_Bub "Mike Bub") say, "Don't slap on every gig", or in songs where it is not appropriate. As well, bluegrass bassists who play slap-style on live shows often slap less on records. Bub and his mentor [Jerry McCoury](/wiki/Jerry_McCoury "Jerry McCoury") rarely do slap bass on recordings. While bassists such as Jack Cook slap bass on the occasional faster "Clinch Mountain Boys song", bassists such as Gene Libbea, [Missy Raines](/wiki/Missy_Raines "Missy Raines"), Jenny Keel, and [Barry Bales](/wiki/Barry_Bales "Barry Bales") [rarely] slap bass.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Bluegrass bassist Mark Schatz, who teaches slap bass in his *Intermediate Bluegrass Bass* DVD acknowledges that slap bass "...has not been stylistically very predominant in the music I have recorded". He notes that "Even in traditional bluegrass slap bass only appears sporadically and most of what I've done has been on the more contemporary side of that (Tony Rice, Tim O'Brien)." Schatz states that he would be "... more likely to use it [slap] in a live situation than on a recording—for a solo or to punctuate a particular place in a song or tune where I wouldn't be obliterating someone's solo".[[55]](#cite_note-55) Another bluegrass method, *Learn to Play Bluegrass Bass*, by Earl Gately, also teaches bluegrass slap bass technique. German bassist Didi Beck plays rapid triplet slaps, as demonstrated in this video.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

## Use in popular music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Use in popular music")]

See also: [List of double bassists in popular music](/wiki/List_of_double_bassists_in_popular_music "List of double bassists in popular music")

In the early 1950s, the upright bass was the standard bass instrument in the emerging style of rock and roll music, [Marshall Lytle](/wiki/Marshall_Lytle "Marshall Lytle") of [Bill Haley & His Comets](/wiki/Bill_Haley_%26_His_Comets "Bill Haley & His Comets") being but one example. In the 1940s, a new style of dance music called [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") developed, incorporating elements of the earlier styles of blues and swing. [Louis Jordan](/wiki/Louis_Jordan "Louis Jordan"), the first innovator of this style, featured an upright bass in his group, the [Tympany Five](/wiki/Tympany_Five "Tympany Five").[[57]](#cite_note-Bartley-57)

The upright bass remained an integral part of pop lineups throughout the 1950s, as the new genre of [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") was built largely upon the model of rhythm and blues, with strong elements also derived from jazz, country, and bluegrass. However, upright bass players using their instruments in these contexts faced inherent problems. They were forced to compete with louder horn instruments (and later amplified [electric guitars](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar")), making bass parts difficult to hear. The upright bass is difficult to amplify in loud concert venue settings, because it can be prone to [feedback](/wiki/Audio_feedback "Audio feedback") *howls*.[[58]](#cite_note-58) As well, the upright bass is large and awkward to transport, which also created transportation problems for touring bands. In some groups, the slap bass was utilized as band percussion in lieu of a drummer; such was the case with Bill Haley & His Saddlemen (the forerunner group to the Comets), which did not use drummers on recordings and live performances until late 1952; prior to this the slap bass was relied on for percussion, including on recordings such as Haley's versions of "[Rock the Joint](/wiki/Rock_the_Joint "Rock the Joint")" and "[Rocket 88](/wiki/Rocket_88 "Rocket 88")".[[59]](#cite_note-59)

In 1951, [Leo Fender](/wiki/Leo_Fender "Leo Fender") released his [Precision Bass](/wiki/Precision_Bass "Precision Bass"), the first commercially successful [electric bass](/wiki/Electric_bass "Electric bass") guitar.[[60]](#cite_note-p-bass-60) The electric bass was easily amplified with its built-in [magnetic pickups](/wiki/Magnetic_pickup "Magnetic pickup"), easily portable (less than a foot longer than an electric guitar), and easier to play in tune than an upright bass, thanks to the metal frets. In the 1960s and 1970s bands were playing at louder volumes and performing in larger venues. The electric bass was able to provide the huge, highly amplified stadium-filling bass tone that the pop and rock music of this era demanded, and the upright bass receded from the limelight of the popular music scene.

Photos of bassist [Miroslav Vitouš](/wiki/Miroslav_Vitou%C5%A1 "Miroslav Vitouš"):

* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Miroslav-Vitous01.jpg/120px-Miroslav-Vitous01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miroslav-Vitous01.jpg)
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Miroslav-Vitous02.jpg/120px-Miroslav-Vitous02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miroslav-Vitous02.jpg)
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Miroslav-Vitous03.jpg/120px-Miroslav-Vitous03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miroslav-Vitous03.jpg)

The upright bass began making a comeback in popular music in the mid-1980s, in part due to a renewed interest in earlier forms of folk and country music, as part of the [roots rock](/wiki/Roots_rock "Roots rock") and [Americana](/wiki/Americana_(music) "Americana (music)") trends. In the 1990s, improvements in pickups and amplifier designs for electro-acoustic horizontal and upright basses made it easier for bassists to get a good, clear amplified tone from an acoustic instrument. Some popular bands decided to anchor their sound with an upright bass instead of an electric bass, such as the [Barenaked Ladies](/wiki/Barenaked_Ladies "Barenaked Ladies"). A trend for "unplugged" performances on [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV"), in which rock bands performed with solely acoustic instruments, further helped to enhance the public's interest in the upright bass and [acoustic bass guitars](/wiki/Acoustic_bass_guitar "Acoustic bass guitar").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/JimCreeggan1.JPG/250px-JimCreeggan1.JPG)](/wiki/File:JimCreeggan1.JPG)

Jim Creeggan of [Barenaked Ladies](/wiki/Barenaked_Ladies "Barenaked Ladies"), pictured at a 2009 show

[Jim Creeggan](/wiki/Jim_Creeggan "Jim Creeggan") of [Barenaked Ladies](/wiki/Barenaked_Ladies "Barenaked Ladies") primarily plays upright bass, although he has increasingly played bass guitar throughout the band's career. [Chris Wyse](/wiki/Chris_Wyse "Chris Wyse") of alternative rock group [Owl](/wiki/Owl_(band) "Owl (band)") uses a combination of electric and double bass. [Athol Guy](/wiki/Athol_Guy "Athol Guy") of the Australian folk/pop group [The Seekers](/wiki/The_Seekers "The Seekers") plays an upright bass. [Shannon Birchall](/wiki/Shannon_Birchall "Shannon Birchall"), of the Australian folk-rock group the [John Butler Trio](/wiki/John_Butler_Trio "John Butler Trio"),[[61]](#cite_note-61) makes extensive use of upright basses, performing extended live solos in songs such as Betterman. On the 2008 album *[In Ear Park](/wiki/In_Ear_Park "In Ear Park")* by the indie/pop band [Department of Eagles](/wiki/Department_of_Eagles "Department of Eagles"), a bowed upright bass is featured quite prominently on the songs "Teenagers" and "In Ear Park". Norwegian ompa-rock band [Kaizers Orchestra](/wiki/Kaizers_Orchestra "Kaizers Orchestra") use the upright bass exclusively both live and on their recordings.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

French contemporary pop duet "What a day" uses double bass extended pizzicato technique with vocals and type writer.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

[Hank Williams III](/wiki/Hank_Williams_III "Hank Williams III")'s bass players (Jason Brown, [Joe Buck](/wiki/Joe_Buck_(musician) "Joe Buck (musician)") and Zach Shedd, most notably) have used upright basses for recording as well as during the country and Hellbilly sets of Hank III's live performances before switching to electric bass for the [Assjack](/wiki/Assjack "Assjack") set.

The late 1970s rockabilly-punk genre of psychobilly continued and expanded upon the rockabilly tradition of slap bass. Bassists such as [Kim Nekroman](/wiki/Kim_Nekroman "Kim Nekroman") and [Geoff Kresge](/wiki/Geoff_Kresge "Geoff Kresge") have developed the ability to play rapid slap bass that in effect turns the bass into a percussion instrument.

## Modern playing styles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Modern playing styles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Double_Bass_during_the_break_%28photo_by_Garry_Knight%29.jpg/250px-Double_Bass_during_the_break_%28photo_by_Garry_Knight%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Double_Bass_during_the_break_(photo_by_Garry_Knight).jpg)

A mid-sized bass amp used to amplify a double bass at a small jazz gig

In popular music genres, the instrument is usually played with [amplification](/wiki/Bass_instrument_amplifier "Bass instrument amplifier") and almost exclusively played with the fingers, *[pizzicato](/wiki/Pizzicato "Pizzicato")* style. The pizzicato style varies between different players and genres. Some players perform with the sides of one, two, or three fingers, especially for walking basslines and slow tempo ballads, because this is purported to create a stronger and more solid tone. Some players use the more nimble tips of the fingers to play fast-moving solo passages or to pluck lightly for quiet tunes. The use of amplification allows the player to have more control over the tone of the instrument, because amplifiers have equalization controls that allow the bassist to accentuate certain frequencies (often the bass frequencies) while de-accentuating some frequencies (often the high frequencies, so that there is less finger noise).

An unamplified acoustic bass's tone is limited by the frequency responsiveness of the instrument's hollow body, which means that the very low pitches may not be as loud as the higher pitches. With an amplifier and equalization devices, a bass player can boost the low frequencies, which changes the frequency response. In addition, the use of an amplifier can increase the sustain of the instrument, which is particularly useful for accompaniment during ballads and for melodic solos with held notes.

In traditional jazz, [swing](/wiki/Swing_(genre) "Swing (genre)"), [polka](/wiki/Polka "Polka"), rockabilly, and psychobilly music, it is sometimes played in the *[slap style](/wiki/Slapping_(music) "Slapping (music)")*. This is a vigorous version of pizzicato where the strings are "slapped" against the fingerboard between the main notes of the bass line, producing a [snare drum](/wiki/Snare_drum "Snare drum")-like percussive sound. The main notes are either played normally or by pulling the string away from the fingerboard and releasing it so that it bounces off the fingerboard, producing a distinctive percussive attack in addition to the expected pitch. Notable slap style bass players, whose use of the technique was often highly syncopated and virtuosic, sometimes interpolated two, three, four, or more slaps in between notes of the bass line.

"Slap style" may have influenced electric bass guitar players [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] who, from the mid-sixties (particularly Larry Graham of Sly and the Family Stone), developed a technique called *[slap and pop](/wiki/Slap-back "Slap-back")* that used the thumb of the plucking hand to hit the string, making a slapping sound but still letting the note ring, and the index or middle finger of the plucking hand to pull the string back so it hits the fretboard, achieving the pop sound described above. [Motown](/wiki/Motown "Motown") bass player [James Jamerson](/wiki/James_Jamerson "James Jamerson") routinely used a double bass for enhancement of the electric bass in post-production ("sweetening") of recorded tracks and vice versa in many instances.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Classical

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Gary_karr.jpg/250px-Gary_karr.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gary_karr.jpg)

Double bass soloist Gary Karr

Some of the most influential contemporary classical double bass players are known as much for their contributions to pedagogy as for their performing skills, such as US bassist [Oscar G. Zimmerman](/wiki/Oscar_G._Zimmerman "Oscar G. Zimmerman") (1910–1987), known for his teaching at the [Eastman School of Music](/wiki/Eastman_School_of_Music "Eastman School of Music") and, for 44 summers at the [Interlochen](/wiki/Interlochen "Interlochen") National Music Camp in [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan") and French bassist [François Rabbath](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Rabbath "François Rabbath") (b. 1931) who developed a new bass method that divided the entire fingerboard into six positions. Bassists noted for their virtuoso solo skills include American pedagogue and performer [Gary Karr](/wiki/Gary_Karr "Gary Karr") (1941-2025), Finnish composer [Teppo Hauta-aho](/wiki/Teppo_Hauta-aho "Teppo Hauta-aho") (b. 1941), Italian composer Fernando Grillo, and US player-composer Edgar Meyer. For a longer list, see the *[List of contemporary classical double bass players](/wiki/List_of_contemporary_classical_double_bass_players "List of contemporary classical double bass players")*.

#### Jazz

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Jazz")]

See also: [List of jazz bassists](/wiki/List_of_jazz_bassists "List of jazz bassists")

Notable jazz bassists from the 1940s to the 1950s included bassist [Jimmy Blanton](/wiki/Jimmy_Blanton "Jimmy Blanton") (1918–1942) whose short tenure in the [Duke Ellington](/wiki/Duke_Ellington "Duke Ellington") Swing band (cut short by his death from [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis")) introduced new melodic and harmonic solo ideas for the instrument; bassist [Ray Brown](/wiki/Ray_Brown_(musician) "Ray Brown (musician)") (1926–2002), known for backing Beboppers [Dizzy Gillespie](/wiki/Dizzy_Gillespie "Dizzy Gillespie"), [Oscar Peterson](/wiki/Oscar_Peterson "Oscar Peterson"), [Art Tatum](/wiki/Art_Tatum "Art Tatum") and [Charlie Parker](/wiki/Charlie_Parker "Charlie Parker"), and forming the [Modern Jazz Quartet](/wiki/Modern_Jazz_Quartet "Modern Jazz Quartet"); [hard bop](/wiki/Hard_bop "Hard bop") bassist Ron Carter (born 1937), who has appeared on 3,500 albums make him one of the most-recorded bassists in jazz history, including LPs by [Thelonious Monk](/wiki/Thelonious_Monk "Thelonious Monk") and [Wes Montgomery](/wiki/Wes_Montgomery "Wes Montgomery") and many [Blue Note Records](/wiki/Blue_Note_Records "Blue Note Records") artists; and [Paul Chambers](/wiki/Paul_Chambers "Paul Chambers") (1935–1969), a member of the [Miles Davis Quintet](/wiki/Miles_Davis_Quintet "Miles Davis Quintet") (including the landmark modal jazz recording *[Kind of Blue](/wiki/Kind_of_Blue "Kind of Blue")*) and many other 1950s and 1960s rhythm sections, was known for his virtuosic [improvisations](/wiki/Improvisation "Improvisation").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Bassist_Christian_McBride.jpg/250px-Bassist_Christian_McBride.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bassist_Christian_McBride.jpg)

[Christian McBride](/wiki/Christian_McBride "Christian McBride") (born 1972), one of the new "young lions" in the jazz scene, has won four [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards").

The experimental post 1960s era, and free jazz and jazz-rock fusion, produced several influential bassists. [Charles Mingus](/wiki/Charles_Mingus "Charles Mingus") (1922–1979), who was also a composer and [bandleader](/wiki/Bandleader "Bandleader"), produced music that fused [hard bop](/wiki/Hard_bop "Hard bop") with black [gospel music](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music"), [free jazz](/wiki/Free_jazz "Free jazz"), and classical music. [Free jazz](/wiki/Free_jazz "Free jazz") and post-bop bassist [Charlie Haden](/wiki/Charlie_Haden "Charlie Haden") (1937–2014) is best known for his long association with saxophonist [Ornette Coleman](/wiki/Ornette_Coleman "Ornette Coleman"), and for his role in the 1970s-era [Liberation Music Orchestra](/wiki/Liberation_Music_Orchestra "Liberation Music Orchestra"), an experimental group. [Eddie Gómez](/wiki/Eddie_G%C3%B3mez "Eddie Gómez") and [George Mraz](/wiki/George_Mraz "George Mraz"), who played with [Bill Evans](/wiki/Bill_Evans "Bill Evans") and [Oscar Peterson](/wiki/Oscar_Peterson "Oscar Peterson"), respectively, and are both acknowledged to have furthered expectations of pizzicato fluency and melodic phrasing. [Fusion](/wiki/Jazz_fusion "Jazz fusion") virtuoso [Stanley Clarke](/wiki/Stanley_Clarke "Stanley Clarke") (born 1951) is notable for his dexterity on both the upright bass and the electric bass. [Terry Plumeri](/wiki/Terry_Plumeri "Terry Plumeri") is noted for his horn-like arco fluency and vocal-sounding tone.

In the 1990s and first decade of the 21st century, one of the new "young lions" was [Christian McBride](/wiki/Christian_McBride "Christian McBride") (born 1972), who has performed with a range of veterans ranging from [McCoy Tyner](/wiki/McCoy_Tyner "McCoy Tyner") to fusion gurus [Herbie Hancock](/wiki/Herbie_Hancock "Herbie Hancock") and [Chick Corea](/wiki/Chick_Corea "Chick Corea"), and who has released albums such as 2003's *[Vertical Vision](/wiki/Vertical_Vision "Vertical Vision")*. Another young bassist of note is [Esperanza Spalding](/wiki/Esperanza_Spalding "Esperanza Spalding") (born 1984) who, at 27 years of age, had already won a [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy "Grammy") for Best New Artist.

[Scott Owen](/wiki/Scott_Owen "Scott Owen"), double bass player for Australian rock band [The Living End](/wiki/The_Living_End "The Living End")

In addition to being a noted classical player, [Edgar Meyer](/wiki/Edgar_Meyer "Edgar Meyer") is well known in [bluegrass](/wiki/Bluegrass_music "Bluegrass music") and [newgrass](/wiki/Progressive_bluegrass "Progressive bluegrass") circles. [Todd Phillips](/wiki/Todd_Phillips "Todd Phillips") is another prominent bluegrass player. Well-known [rockabilly](/wiki/Rockabilly "Rockabilly") bassists include [Bill Black](/wiki/Bill_Black "Bill Black"), [Marshall Lytle](/wiki/Marshall_Lytle "Marshall Lytle") (with [Bill Haley & His Comets](/wiki/Bill_Haley_%26_His_Comets "Bill Haley & His Comets")) and [Lee Rocker](/wiki/Lee_Rocker "Lee Rocker") (with 1980s-era rockabilly revivalists the [Stray Cats](/wiki/Stray_Cats "Stray Cats")).

Notable rockabilly revivalists and psychobilly performers from the 1990s and first decade of the 21st century include [Scott Owen](/wiki/Scott_Owen "Scott Owen") (from the Australian band [The Living End](/wiki/The_Living_End "The Living End")), [Jimbo Wallace](/wiki/Jimbo_Wallace "Jimbo Wallace") (from the US band [Reverend Horton Heat](/wiki/Reverend_Horton_Heat "Reverend Horton Heat")), [Kim Nekroman](/wiki/Kim_Nekroman "Kim Nekroman") ([Nekromantix](/wiki/Nekromantix "Nekromantix")), [Patricia Day](/wiki/Patricia_Day "Patricia Day") ([HorrorPops](/wiki/HorrorPops "HorrorPops")), [Geoff Kresge](/wiki/Geoff_Kresge "Geoff Kresge") ([Tiger Army](/wiki/Tiger_Army "Tiger Army"), ex-[AFI](/wiki/AFI_(band) "AFI (band)")). [Willie Dixon](/wiki/Willie_Dixon "Willie Dixon") (1915–1992) was one of the most notable figures in the history of [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues"). In addition to being an upright bassist, he wrote dozens of R&B hits and worked as a producer. He also plays bass on numerous [Chuck Berry](/wiki/Chuck_Berry "Chuck Berry")'s rock and roll hits. Many other rockabilly bands like El Rio Trio (from the Netherlands) also use this instrument in their work. See also the [List of double bassists in popular music](/wiki/List_of_double_bassists_in_popular_music "List of double bassists in popular music").

## Pedagogy and training

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Pedagogy and training")]

The pedagogy and training for the double bass varies widely by genre and country. Classical double bass has a history of pedagogy dating back several centuries, including teaching manuals, studies, and progressive exercises that help students to develop the endurance and accuracy of the left hand, and control for the bowing hand. Classical training methods vary by country: many of the major European countries are associated with specific methods (e.g., the [Édouard Nanny](/wiki/%C3%89douard_Nanny "Édouard Nanny") method in France or the [Franz Simandl](/wiki/Franz_Simandl "Franz Simandl") method in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany")). In classical training, the majority of the instruction for the right hand focuses on the production of bowing tone; little time is spent studying the varieties of pizzicato tone.

In contrast, in genres that mainly or exclusively use pizzicato (plucking), such as jazz and blues, a great deal of time and effort is focused on learning the varieties of different pizzicato styles used for music of different styles of tempi. For example, in jazz, aspiring bassists have to learn how to perform a wide range of pizzicato tones, including using the sides of the fingers to create a full, deep sound for ballads, using the tips of the fingers for fast walking basslines or solos, and performing a variety of percussive *ghost notes* by raking muted or partially muted strings.

### Formal training

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Formal training")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Nobel_Peace_Price_Concert_2009_Esperanza_Spalding1.jpg/250px-Nobel_Peace_Price_Concert_2009_Esperanza_Spalding1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nobel_Peace_Price_Concert_2009_Esperanza_Spalding1.jpg)

Jazz singer/bassist [Esperanza Spalding](/wiki/Esperanza_Spalding "Esperanza Spalding") performing on 10 December 2009 at the [Nobel Peace Prize Concert](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") of 2009

Of all of the genres, classical and jazz have the most established and comprehensive systems of instruction and training. In the classical milieu, children can begin taking private lessons on the instrument and performing in children's or youth orchestras. Teens who aspire to becoming professional classical bassists can continue their studies in a variety of formal training settings, including colleges, conservatories, and universities. Colleges offer certificates and diplomas in bass performance.

[Conservatories](/wiki/Music_school "Music school"), which are the standard musical training system in France and in Quebec (Canada) provide lessons and amateur orchestral experience for double bass players. Universities offer a range of double bass programs, including bachelor's degrees, Master of Music degrees, and [Doctor of Musical Arts](/wiki/Doctor_of_Musical_Arts "Doctor of Musical Arts") degrees. As well, there are a variety of other training programs such as classical summer camps and orchestral, opera, or chamber music training festivals, which give students the opportunity to play a wide range of music.

Bachelor's degrees in bass performance (referred to as [B.Mus.](/wiki/B.Mus. "B.Mus.") or B.M.) are four-year programs that include individual bass lessons, amateur orchestra experience, and a sequence of courses in music history, music theory, and liberal arts courses (e.g., English literature), which give the student a more well-rounded education. Usually, bass performance students perform several recitals of solo double bass music, such as concertos, sonatas, and Baroque suites.

Master of music degrees ([M.mus.](/wiki/M.mus. "M.mus.")) in double bass performance consist of private lessons, ensemble experience, coaching in playing orchestral double bass parts, and graduate courses in music history and music theory, along with one or two solo recitals. A Master's degree in music (referred to as an [M.Mus.](/wiki/M.Mus. "M.Mus.") or M.M.) is often a required credential for people who wish to become a professor of double bass at a university or conservatory.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/TimothyBCobb.jpg/250px-TimothyBCobb.jpg)](/wiki/File:TimothyBCobb.jpg)

Manhattan School of Music professor [Timothy Cobb](/wiki/Timothy_Cobb "Timothy Cobb") teaching a bass lesson in the late 2000s. His bass has a low C extension with a metal "machine" with buttons for playing the pitches on the extension.

Doctor of Musical Arts (referred to as D.M.A., DMA, D.Mus.A. or A.Mus.D.) degrees in double bass performance provide an opportunity for advanced study at the highest artistic and pedagogical level, requiring usually an additional 54+ credit hours beyond a master's degree (which is about 30+ credits beyond a bachelor's degree). For this reason, admission is highly selective. Examinations in music history, music theory, ear training/dictation, and an entrance examination-recital, are required. Students perform a number of recitals (around six), including a lecture-recital with an accompanying doctoral dissertation, advanced coursework, and a minimum B average are other typical requirements of a D.M.A. program.

Throughout the early history of jazz, double bass players either learned the instrument informally, or from getting classical training early on, as in the case of Ron Carter and Charles Mingus. In the 1980s and 1990s, colleges and universities began to introduce diplomas and degrees in jazz performance. Students in jazz diploma or Bachelor of Music programs take individual bass lessons, get experience in small jazz combos with coaching from an experienced player, and play in jazz big bands. As with classical training programs, jazz programs also include classroom courses in music history and music theory. In a jazz program, these courses focus on the different eras of jazz history. such as Swing, Bebop, and fusion. The theory courses focus on the musical skills used in jazz improvisation and in jazz comping (accompanying) and the composition of jazz tunes. There are also jazz summer camps and training festivals/seminars, which offer students the chance to learn new skills and styles.

### Informal training

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Informal training")]

In other genres, such as blues, rockabilly, and psychobilly, the pedagogical systems and training sequences are not as formalized and institutionalized. There are not degrees in blues bass performance, or conservatories offering multiple-year diplomas in rockabilly bass. However, there are a range of books, playing methods, and, since the 1990s, instructional DVDs (e.g., on how to play rockabilly-style slap bass). As such, performers in these other genres tend to come from a variety of routes, including informal learning by using bass method books or DVDs, taking private lessons and coaching, and learning from records and CDs. In some cases, blues or rockabilly bassists may have obtained some initial training through the classical or jazz pedagogy systems (e.g., youth orchestra or high school big band). In genres such as tango, which use a lot of bowed passages and jazz-style pizzicato lines, the bassists tend to come from classical or jazz training routes.

## Careers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Careers")]

Careers in double bass vary widely by genre and by region or country. Most bassists earn their living from a mixture of performance and teaching jobs. The first step to getting most performance jobs is by playing at an [audition](/wiki/Audition "Audition"). In some styles of music, such as jazz-oriented stage bands, bassists may be asked to [sight read](/wiki/Sight_reading "Sight reading") printed music or perform standard pieces (e.g., a [jazz standard](/wiki/Jazz_standard "Jazz standard") such as *Now's the Time*) with an ensemble. Similarly, in a rock or blues band, auditionees may be asked to play various rock or blues standards. An upright bassist auditioning for a blues band might be asked to play in a Swing-style walking [bassline](/wiki/Bassline "Bassline"), a rockabilly-style ["slapping"](/wiki/Slap_bass "Slap bass") bassline (in which the strings are percussively struck against the fingerboard) and a 1950s ballad with long held notes. A person auditioning for a role as a bassist in some styles of pop or rock music may be expected to demonstrate the ability to perform [harmony vocals](/wiki/Harmony_vocals "Harmony vocals") as a [backup singer](/wiki/Backup_singer "Backup singer"). In some pop and rock groups, the bassist may be asked to play other instruments from time to time, such as electric bass, keyboards or acoustic guitar. The ability to play electric bass is widely expected in country groups, in case the band is performing a [classic rock](/wiki/Classic_rock "Classic rock") or new country song.

### Classical music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Double_bass&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Classical music")]

In [classical music](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music"), bassists audition for playing jobs in orchestras and for admission into university or Conservatory programs or degrees. At a classical bass audition, the performer typically plays a movement from a [J.S. Bach suite for solo cello](/wiki/J.S._Bach_suite_for_solo_cello "J.S. Bach suite for solo cello") or a movement from a bass concerto and a variety of excerpts from the orchestral literature. The excerpts are typically the most technically challenging parts of bass parts and bass solos from the orchestral literature. Some of the most commonly requested orchestral excerpts at bass auditions are from [Beethoven](/wiki/Beethoven "Beethoven")'s Symphonies Nos. 5, 7 and 9; [Strauss](/wiki/Strauss "Strauss")'s *[Ein Heldenleben](/wiki/Ein_Heldenleben "Ein Heldenleben")* and *Don Juan*; [Mozart](/wiki/Mozart "Mozart")'s Symphonies Nos. 35, 39 and 40; [Brahms](/wiki/Brahms "Brahms")' Symphonies Nos. 1 and 2; [Stravinsky](/wiki/Stravinsky "Stravinsky")'s Pulcinella; [Shostakovich](/wiki/Shostakovich "Shostakovich")'s Symphony No. 5; [Ginastera](/wiki/Ginastera "Ginastera")'s *Variaciones Concertante*; [Tchaikovsky](/wiki/Tchaikovsky "Tchaikovsky")'s Symphony No. 4; [Mahler](/wiki/Mahler "Mahler")'s Symphony No. 2; J. S. Bach's Suite No. 2 in B; [Berlioz](/wiki/Berlioz "Berlioz")'s *[Symphonie Fantastique](/wiki/Symphonie_Fantastique "Symphonie Fantastique")*, [Mendelssohn](/wiki/Mendelssohn "Mendelssohn")'s Symphony No. 4; and the bass solos from [Verdi](/wiki/Verdi "Verdi")'s opera *[Otello](/wiki/Otello "Otello")*, [Mahler](/wiki/Mahler "Mahler")'s Symphony No. 1, [Britten](/wiki/Britten "Britten")'s *[The Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra](/wiki/The_Young_Person%27s_Guide_to_the_Orchestra "The Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra")* and [Prokofiev](/wiki/Prokofiev "Prokofiev")'s *[Lieutenant Kije Suite](/wiki/Lieutenant_Kije_Suite "Lieutenant Kije Suite")*.[[64]](#cite_note-64)
